item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
The first image that was used in this article has been replaced with the following image, which is cc-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0):

Source: pxfuel
License: CC0
Title: N/A
Author: N/A
Release year: N/A

The second image has been removed as it's owned and copyrighted by Filiberto Caponi as indicated here, and is thus non-compliant.

EDIT: The second image that was used in this article has been replaced with an image that's cc-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0) and is sourced as follows:

Title: 1341-2-new.jpg
Author(s): Shankar S., Bradford Cody Williams, Elogee FishTruck, Roget
Release year: 2020
Note: Created by Elogee FishTruck utilizing the images below as components in the final image, before being edited by Roget as well.

Source: Flickr
License: CC BY 2.0
Title: Night jungle trek- dark and mysterious
Author: Shankar S.

Source: Wikimedia Commons
License: CC BY-SA 3.0
Title: "Zabar" Rod Puppet.jpg
Author: Bradford Cody Williams

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A jungle in a jar would be fairly uninteresting. The addition of sentient creatures being produced kicked it up a notch and the catastrophic breach journal made it great for me. Perhaps a little melodramatic but still informative of how horrific the scip can get. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So, how did the Foundation recover the SCP? I seems like the pretty impenetrable fortress of these strange plants would keep them out. Did they blast a hole in the roof or something? Does a jungle in a can exposed to outside air just distintegrate? Was it not recovered? It seems th article implies it was recovered. Overall though, I like this SCP. Especially the Addendum. Really ties it all in for me.

item.title: Suggested edits:
item.content:encoded:
- The Class-D escapes and the Foundation finds out but decides they don't care.

- The scientist sets off the on-site nuke instead (cause it's obvious that they should go for the nuke instead of the exits).

Note: This deserves a MOVIE. Upboated.

item.title: Reforestation?
item.content:encoded: I feel like whoever made this or whatever spawned this jar made it for the purpose of reforestation if our current jungles are all wiped out but that is just my interpretation.

item.title: Reforestation?
item.content:encoded: I feel like whoever made this or whatever spawned this jar made it for the purpose of reforestation if our current jungles are all wiped out but that is just my interpretation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Couldn't this be classified as Safe?
It looks like there are no problems as long as nobody opens the thing. Containment procedures: keep the lid on. Done.
Am I missing something?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, you can also PM the author if you had a mind to in these cases, though this is pretty minor for that. Also, since It's a written document, I think we can assume that's just how the author of the document wrote it. If Roget sees the post, he can decide whether to change it or not.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I hope god gives me the strength to protect my staff and get us out of this mess safely.

Hey, guys, I'm brand new to the site. I THINK noticed a slight error in the article. Since the word "god" is being used as a name in this context, it should be capitalized ("God"). Is this something that needs to be changed, or is it to be assumed that Dr. Boyd misspelled this in his writing and that this was done on purpose by the author?

Is this even the right place to ask this kind of a question?

item.title: Dirt to dirt
item.content:encoded: Incident 1341-A and D-0981 make the SCP for me. They highlight the unknown and usually dangerous consequences of testing without proper supervision or contingencies. I also like how a D-class personnel for once wasn't simply cannon fodder. Until the end I had presumed them dead due to the unfolding events, but somehow they managed to survive and ironically to preach of site staff's errors. Raises a lot of questions about his task and the affects of SCP 1341. Also Dr. Boyd's paranoia at the end was quite fitting too.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Timer Expired. Edits made as discussed below. Author may revert at any time.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team

+ Show Block
- Hide Block

SCP-1341 is directly referenced in SCP-1513, with the belief that both plant based anomalies share a similar point of origin from the same group/entity. It would make sense then to have that reference flow both ways and have 1341 link back to 1513. As such, I am recommending the following edits:

Changing this:

Instances of SCP-1341-2 resemble vaguely simian bipeds, and are not hostile unless provoked. Instances of SCP-1341-2 exhibit behavior patterns consistent with those of wild chimpanzees. When the population of SCP-1341-2 has reached between twenty and thirty, the area affected by SCP-1341 will expand to fill the largest enclosed space possible. The root structure of the plants within SCP-1341 will spread through the walls, ceiling, and floors of any artificial structure it has been placed within. New plants will begin to grow from these root structures, until SCP-1341 has completely assimilated the structure.

To this:

Instances of SCP-1341-2 resemble vaguely simian bipeds, and are not hostile unless provoked. Instances of SCP-1341-2 exhibit behavior patterns consistent with those of wild chimpanzees1. When the population of SCP-1341-2 has reached between twenty and thirty, the area affected by SCP-1341 will expand to fill the largest enclosed space possible. The root structure of the plants within SCP-1341 will spread through the walls, ceiling, and floors of any artificial structure it has been placed within. New plants will begin to grow from these root structures, until SCP-1341 has completely assimilated the structure.

If anyone has other/better examples that could be used here please leave a comment saying so!

Footnotes
1. Similarity between SCP-1341 and SCP-1513's ability to produce fauna from its flora has been noted. Investigation into a shared origin of the two anomalies is ongoing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
D-0981 is alive. Sometimes he talks over the PA system. He rants and raves about how we let this happen, we let the base fall and become a "pit of weeds and depravity" as he puts it

Just out of curiosity, is that a Heart of Darkness reference because I can see future addendum heading this page in that direction.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is actually kind of a good point… the only Devil's Advocate arguments I can think of for keeping the Euclid classification are:

  1. if the lid should somehow work its way loose1 and go unoticed for a while, you could discover this thing in full breach while taking inventory.
  2. It is made of glass, and if it breaks there is no guarantee you could just mop the stuff back into another jam jar- there might be something special about that one
  3. There is a sapient component to the anomaly when uncontained, and D-0981 indicates that there may be some form of mind-altering component as well.
Footnotes
1. say by the combined efforts of heat contraction/expansion, resonant vibrations from the surrounding area, and internal pressure

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hrmm… wouldn't this thing be considered safe these days? I mean… so long as the lid doesn't come off, nothing really happens.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Initial experimentation on SCP-1341 was initiated.

That sentence makes me cringe a little.
other than that (and maybe adding some aquisition/recovery elaboration), top notch stuff.

+1 to you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The second picture is actually the "Arquata del Tronto's alien."
Basically, in 1993 in Arquata del Tronto, Italy, Mr. Filiberto Caponi found that adorable creature next to his house and decided to take some pictures of it.
It's actually a famous story here in Italy. We see that thing on TV every year :3

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have no idea how they did it, I wrote this as like, my fifth thing on the site, and it used to make me cringe until I rewrote it a bit. If it makes any difference, I like to imagine they liberated the site with the power of friendship and massive use of chemical defoliants.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Overall it was decent, but to me the biggest hole in it is the lack of details covering the recovery of SCP-1341 following the incident. even a single line referencing the use of some 'super' defoliant that was effective where others were not would give closure. As the article stands it gives the impression that the incident site was just written off and abandoned and left overgrown.
I have to leave a no-vote as is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I tried to smooth out the language in the description here, because it was painful for me to read

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really liked it. I felt it the entire way through, and it was a very fun read. I get what other people are saying, but this article really hit me.

Of course, like some people said, the article was leading to the site's destruction, but the survival log gave me the chills, and I liked the last 'I'm going to take myself out first.' Very The Most Dangerous Game.

It also brought back memories of The Lorax:

"And, under the trees, I saw Brown Bar-ba-loots
frisking about in their Bar-ba-loot suits
as they played in the shade and ate Truffula fruits."

+1 for sentiments.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Did they ever have a budget that wasn't limited?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
*sigh*

The stuff that's good here (like the jungle descriptions) isn't original. And the rest is… Eh. *Shrug* It's competently written, but that isn't enough.

-1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Bolvangar?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The super-tough trees are a bit much. Long-term observation of the destroyed site? Vote withheld for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This was during initial testing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wait, why were they doing some test involving a D-class's interaction with it? I checked a few times, and, unless I'm missing something, there's no mention of it having odd effects on the people around/using it in the description.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fixed "criteria". Next time you see some super minor technical error like that, it's fine to go fix it if there's not an indication that it might be intentional.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Actually, I know one little girl, about 4 years old, named Lyra. Don't know why, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I can see how this is superficially similar to jumanji, but I think that in terms of how they function, and the actual effect, they're quite different.

Also, if we have lawn gnomes that make plastic ecosystems, talking nukes that want to explode, robo-dude, pesterbot, and a myriad of other, sillier articles, I fail to see how this is too "silly."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's Jumanji. In a jar. Downvoted because the idea is silly and not aligned with what /usually/ is kept with the Foundation. Doesn't mean that its bad; just means I don't likes it. Jus' sayin'. Jus'.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I thought the log was a little over the top. If nothing else, no one besides magical ponies is ever named Lyra (so glad someone made that joke already). I really like the article in spite of the log though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well-freaking-done sir. Love the concept, pictures, and execution. PLUS ALL THE 1's!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I knew exactly what it'd be when I clicked that link. That being said, dammit all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Go for it man. I'd love to see one of my articles be used in a tale.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Am I the only one getting a strong Jumanji vibe from this?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Still, for an organization like the Foundation, a week without any communication whatsoever from a Site should set off alarm bells, let alone a month. Even presumably understood Safe or Euclid objects can demonstrate unexpected properties that would make them much more dangerous, not to mention attacks from the Foundation's numerous enemies.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Awww you nixed the stars and rivers part.

Only complaint is that "criteria" is a plural word.

Good work! +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
From what I could gather, the Site was likely host to low-level Safe or possibly "mild Euclid" objects and simple research. I doubt they'd need a dead-man-switch or constant communication, that would be mostly a thing where there were Keter or as-yet-unknown objects.

There's also the fact that Outpost Delta itself may have been the one needing monthly communications, not the Site. *shrug*

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That possesses 2 toes, 3 fingers, a bloated torso, and spherical head with black eyes and semi-defined facial features?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Im PRETTY sure that pic is of some kind of mummified child. Like, a South American mountain mummy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My only real problem is the second picture, it reminds me of a cheap 'goon' villain from Power Rangers (back in the 90s when they had a limited budget).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So. Much. Upvote.

I love the idea of this any the writing style was also great.

One question, and an idea:

1. Can I use this in a Tale

2. Maybe if it spawned other animals, bugs or something as well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What is that second picture even from? That's crazy.

Also I still think the off-limits area for arboreal things should be elaborated on. That could be a potentially big area.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Quite well-done, and that second picture was a good ace in the hole. Also:

Lyra…I don't know what happened to Lyra.

One could only guess where she is now...

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It works. Largely because of the inexplicably creepy second picture. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The following document was recovered from Site ██, after the site did not make its monthly scheduled radio contacts with Outpost Delta.

I can't believe that a Foundation site would stay out of contact with the rest of the Foundation for a month at a time. If I were an O5 I'd want each site to send in a status update at least once a day.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's a good start, but it's pretty predictable once you know that a Site stopped communications. There's not much mystery about what's going to happen to the facility and staff.

Maybe if the fruit-people didn't show up during initial testing and instead were discovered by the unfortunate researchers in the overrun facility.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Someone get me out of this pit of weeds and depravity.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have been holding on to that picture ever since my first(failed) scp, and now I finally got to use it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That second picture spooked me pretty good. The article itself is good too, so +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I normally like ones like these, but this one just isn't compelling me for some reason. I know that's horribly unspecific and unhelpful, but there you go.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Double fix'd

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: fix'd

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: "Albert got stuck in some vines, and we couldn't get him out. After about a week, they had grown over him completely." - This happened in the 4 days since the vines overgrew the windows? Might want to edit the dates of the journal.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-1341 is to be held in a 3x3x4 shed

3x3x4 what? Hands? Feet? Centimeters?
